title: Low Back Pain
authors: Steven P. Cohen, Eric J. Wang

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.title: Epidemiology and Economic Impact of Low Back Pain
items.content: Low back pain (LBP) is a major contributor to disability worldwide, leading in work-related disabilities across industrialized nations. A significant portion of the U.S. population experiences LBP annually, resulting in substantial medical costs.
keyPoints: 28-34% of Americans experience LBP in the past 3 months.
>57 million unique patient visits for LBP annually in the U.S.
All-cause medical costs exceed $300 billion per year in the U.S.
Risk factors include female sex, African-American race, older age, unemployment, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.title: Categorization of Low Back Pain: Nociceptive, Neuropathic, and Nociplastic
items.content: Understanding the categorization of LBP is crucial for treatment decisions. Nociceptive pain results from tissue-damaging stimuli and worsens with activity. Neuropathic pain arises from nervous system damage, presenting with sensory abnormalities. Nociplastic pain involves abnormal pain signal processing without tissue damage.
keyPoints: Nociceptive LBP is common and progressive, worsening with stress on structures.
Neuropathic pain includes paresthesias, numbness, allodynia, and focal neurologic findings.
Nociplastic pain involves diffuse pain, superficial tenderness, and deviations from normal neuroanatomy.
>10-55% of chronic LBP patients report neuropathic qualities.
10-20% of the overall back pain population have nociplastic pain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP
items.title: Prognosis of Acute vs. Chronic Mechanical Low Back Pain (LBP)
items.content: The prognosis for mechanical low back pain is significantly influenced by whether the condition is acute or chronic. Acute nonradicular pain shows a high rate of persistence over time, with substantial percentages experiencing ongoing pain at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months post-onset.
keyPoints: Acute mechanical LBP has an 80% (95% CI, 61–100%) chance of persisting at 1 month.
67% (95% CI, 50–83%) experience pain at 3 months.
57% (95% CI, 46–68%) have ongoing symptoms at 6 months.
65% (95% CI, 54–75%) report pain at 12 months.
Chronic LBP shows stagnant improvement rates with few dramatic improvements after 6 months.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: RADICULAR LBP
items.title: Persistence and Progression in Radicular Low Back Pain (LBP)
items.content: Radicular low back pain often persists over time, with a significant percentage of patients experiencing ongoing symptoms at 6 months to 1 year. Herniated disks typically resorb within 2 years but may reherniate, while spinal stenosis tends to remain stable without regression.
keyPoints: 15-40% of individuals with radicular pain have persistent symptoms at 6 months to 1 year.
Herniated disks usually resorb within 2 years but can reherniate.
Spinal stenosis patients often remain stable, though progression is possible.
Risk factors for poor outcomes include greater disease burden, older age, psychopathology, poor job satisfaction, and secondary gain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Myofascial Pain in Low Back Pain
items.content: Myofascial pain arises from muscles, ligaments, and fascia due to their nociceptors. It is often associated with higher levels of neuropeptides, neurotransmitters, inflammatory cytokines, and vascular abnormalities at active trigger points compared to normal muscle.
keyPoints: Muscles, ligaments, and fascia are sources of mechanical pain in the spine.
Higher levels of substance P, bradykinin, norepinephrine, 5-hydroxytryptamine, tumor necrosis factor α, interleukins, lower pH, and vascular abnormalities found at active trigger points.
Trigger points more challenging to palpate in low back than mid-back or neck.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.title: Discogenic Pain: Prevalence and Clinical Features
items.content: Disk degeneration accounts for a significant portion of axial LBP cases, with nerve fibers populating the inner annulus in degenerated disks. Clinically, discogenic pain worsens with sitting or bending forward and often radiates nondermatomally.
keyPoints: Disk degeneration reported to account for 26–42% of patients with axial LBP.
Nerve fibers populate the inner annulus in degenerated disks.
Pain worsened by sitting or bending, bilateral more than facet or SI joint pain, often radiates nondermatomally.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.title: Facet Joint Pain: Diagnosis and Characteristics
items.content: Facet joint pain affects 10–15% of individuals with axial LBP, often unilateral and paraspinal. It is diagnosed via anesthetic blocks but has high false-positive rates.
keyPoints: Facet joint pain affects approximately 10–15% of individuals with axial LBP.
Pain more likely to be unilateral and paraspinal.
Diagnosis made via anesthetic blocks, which have high false-positive rates.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.title: High Sensitivity and Specificity of Provocative Tests for SI Joint Pain
items.content: A battery of three or more provocative tests on physical examination, such as Patrick's test, Gaenslen's test, SI joint distraction, and compression, has high sensitivity and specificity for detecting intra-articular sacroiliac (SI) joint pain.
keyPoints: Provocative tests include Patrick's test, Gaenslen's test, SI joint distraction, and compression.
High sensitivity and specificity for detecting intra-articular SI joint pain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Lumbar Disk Herniation
items.content: Lumbar disk herniation has an annual incidence of about 1% with a point prevalence between 1.5 and 4%. Asymptomatic cases are more common, ranging from 29 to 43%, increasing with age. Symptoms include low back pain (LBP) radiating into the lower leg in a dermatomal distribution, sensory deficits, neurologic motor deficits (25–30%), and occasionally asymmetrical or diminished reflexes, especially when L4 or S1 is involved (<20%). The straight leg raising test has ~80% sensitivity for L5 and more caudad nerve roots.
keyPoints: Annual incidence of symptomatic lumbar disk herniation: about 1%
Point prevalence: between 1.5 and 4%
Asymptomatic disk herniation prevalence: 29 to 43%, increasing with age
Symptoms include LBP radiating into the lower leg, sensory deficits, neurologic motor deficits (25–30%)
Diminished reflexes especially when L4 or S1 is involved (<20%)
Straight leg raising test sensitivity ~80% for L5 and more caudad nerve roots
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.title: Identifying Red Flags in Low Back Pain
items.content: History and physical examination can help identify patients who need further diagnostic workup or advanced therapies, including surgery. Specific signs such as midline tenderness may indicate ligamentous injury, while nonorganic signs like overreaction suggest underlying psychopathology.
keyPoints: Midline tenderness may indicate ligamentous injury.
Nonorganic signs (e.g., overreaction) are associated with treatment failure and may signify underlying psychopathology.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.title: Gait Observations in Low Back Pain
items.content: Observation of gait can provide clues to nonspinal pathology. For example, a steppage gait or foot drop may suggest peroneal neuropathy, large herniated disk, Guillain-Barré syndrome, multiple sclerosis, or another neurologic condition.
keyPoints: Steppage gait or foot drop can indicate peroneal neuropathy, large herniated disk, Guillain-Barré syndrome, multiple sclerosis, or other neurologic conditions.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.title: Diagnostic Tests for Radicular Pain
items.content: Specific tests are more specific for radicular than axial pain. The straight leg raising test has high sensitivity but variable specificity for L5-S2 nerve root involvement, while the crossed straight leg raising test demonstrates high specificity.
keyPoints: Straight leg raising test: High sensitivity, variable specificity for L5-S2 nerve root involvement.
Crossed straight leg raising test: >85% specificity but low sensitivity.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.title: Identifying Red Flags in Low Back Pain (LBP)
items.content: Red flags are critical indicators suggesting serious underlying conditions that require urgent attention to prevent permanent neurologic deficits. Despite their presence not significantly reducing the likelihood of a non-emergency, they help identify potential spinal or nonspinal pathologies.
keyPoints: 92.6% of LBP patients endorsed at least one red flag.
Most common red flag: night pain (58.1%).
Red flags can indicate serious conditions like cauda equina syndrome, infectious diseases, and neoplasms.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.title: Guidelines for Imaging in Low Back Pain Management
items.content: Imaging should be reserved for cases with red flags or progressive neurologic deficits. While MRI is the gold standard for soft tissue evaluation, its use must be judicious, especially since imaging findings do not always correlate with symptom severity.
keyPoints: Advanced imaging lacks specificity; symptoms often don't correlate with imaging results.
MRI is preferred for detecting soft tissue abnormalities and active inflammation.
CT offers better spatial contrast and sensitivity for bone abnormalities (>90% sensitivity).
Imaging decisions should consider clinical context, not solely rely on findings.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS
items.title: Role of Electromyography and Nerve Conduction Studies in Diagnosing Radicular Pain
items.content: Electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies are useful for identifying peripheral sources of nerve and muscle injury, especially when there is multiple dermatomal involvement or atypical extremity pain. These tests help resolve conflicts between symptoms and imaging findings, particularly in cases with transitional anatomy or aberrant innervation.
keyPoints: Transitional anatomy (lumbarization or sacralization) present in 15–35% of the population is linked to increased back pain prevalence.
Sensitivity for radicular pain without focal neurologic findings ranges from 36 to 64%; with abnormal neurologic examination, it increases to 51 to 86%.
Specificity of electrodiagnostic testing varies between 50 and 60%.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS

items.subtopic: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS
items.title: Use and Limitations of Selective Nerve Root Blocks in Diagnosing Radiculopathy
items.content: Selective nerve root blocks (SNRBs) are used to identify symptomatic nerve roots when cases are ambiguous. They involve blocking a single nerve root, avoiding epidural spread, which is crucial for maintaining specificity. High-volume injections (>0.5 mL) can reduce the specificity of SNRB.
keyPoints: No randomized studies on the effect of SNRB on postsurgical decompression outcomes; retrospective studies show modest correlation with pain relief post-surgery.
Systematic review found 93% sensitivity and 26% specificity for detecting radiculopathy using SNRB, based on low-quality studies.
SNRB is not considered cost-effective as an addition to routine presurgical workup.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Optimizing Nonpharmacologic Modalities for Acute LBP
items.content: Acute low back pain (LBP) often resolves within 6 weeks, allowing nonpharmacologic treatments like physical therapy and core-strengthening exercises to be effective without the need for imaging or prescription analgesics.
keyPoints: Most cases of acute LBP resolve within 6 weeks.
Over-the-counter analgesics combined with targeted physical therapy, core-strengthening exercises, and education are often sufficient.
Imaging is typically not required in the initial management of acute LBP.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Pharmacologic Management of Chronic LBP
items.content: For chronic LBP, defined as symptoms persisting for more than 3 months, NSAIDs, nonbenzodiazepine muscle relaxants, and certain antidepressants are recommended. Acetaminophen is not advised as a first-line agent due to insufficient analgesic efficacy.
keyPoints: Chronic LBP is defined by symptoms persisting for >3 months.
Pharmacologic options include NSAIDs, nonbenzodiazepine muscle relaxants, and antidepressants like duloxetine.
Acetaminophen is unlikely to provide significant analgesia for back pain and is not recommended as a first-line agent.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Psychological Therapies in Chronic LBP Management
items.content: Chronic back pain often coexists with psychiatric conditions such as depression and anxiety. Psychological therapies like cognitive-behavioral therapy can be beneficial, especially for chronic pain, though benefits may diminish without ongoing follow-up.
keyPoints: High co-prevalence of psychopathology in chronic back pain: depression (33-67%), anxiety (10-30%).
Psychological therapies include cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and biofeedback.
Evidence supports short-term benefits on pain and function; long-term benefits require ongoing follow-up.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.title: Integrative Medicine for Back Pain: Efficacy and Limitations
items.content: Integrative medicine therapies, including acupuncture, massage, and spinal manipulation, offer modest benefits for managing acute and chronic low back pain (LBP). These treatments are often short-lived without continued therapy. Specialists may provide more effective care than generalists due to their greater knowledge base.
keyPoints: Acupuncture is slightly more effective than sham acupuncture but has modest, short-term effects.
Spinal manipulation offers small benefits for acute and chronic back pain compared to no treatment.
Massage can help with acute and chronic pain involving soft tissue symptoms, though benefits are short-lived.
There is mixed evidence supporting the benefit of spinal manipulation as an add-on therapy.
No definitive evidence supports one form of integrative treatment over others.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.title: Interventional Pain Procedures for Axial Low Back Pain
items.content: Interventional pain procedures can provide analgesia and improve physical function in axial LBP when surgical options are not suitable. These procedures target structures like facet joints, SI joints, intervertebral disks, vertebrae, or paraspinal muscles and ligaments.
keyPoints: Most cases of LBP cannot be attributed to a single anatomic source (nonspecific LBP).
A thorough history, physical examination, and appropriate imaging are crucial for identifying targets for interventional procedures.
Interventional pain procedures are often performed fluoroscopically or with ultrasound.
Patients may experience pain from multiple structures simultaneously, complicating diagnosis and treatment.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.title: Diagnosing and Treating Facetogenic Pain with Medial Branch Blocks and RFA
items.content: Facet joint pain accounts for 10–15% of axial low back pain (LBP) cases, increasing with age. Diagnostic medial branch blocks involve local anesthetic administration to the medial branch nerves innervating facet joints suspected of causing axial pain. Significant improvement in pain and physical function following these blocks confirms facetogenic pain diagnosis, allowing subsequent radiofrequency ablation (RFA) for treatment.
keyPoints: Facet joint pain comprises 10–15% of axial LBP cases.
Diagnosis confirmed by significant improvement post-medial branch block.
Radiofrequency ablation can be used following confirmation.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.title: SI Joint Pain Diagnosis and Management Strategies
items.content: The sacroiliac (SI) joints are the primary source of pain in 20–35% of suspected lower axial LBP cases. SI joint pain is often unilateral, occurring below the L5 level. A low-volume intraarticular injection serves as a diagnostic standard. RFA may be considered for extraarticular SI joint ligament pain if injections provide only transient relief. Intraarticular pathology or malalignment might necessitate minimally invasive fusion techniques.
keyPoints: SI joints are primary in 20–35% of lower axial LBP cases.
SI pain is more likely unilateral and below the L5 level.
Low-volume intraarticular injection is diagnostic standard.
RFA for extraarticular ligament pain if injections offer transient relief.
Minimally invasive fusion may be indicated for refractory intraarticular pathology.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.title: Indications for Vertebral Augmentation in Acute Compression Fractures
items.content: Vertebral augmentation, including vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty, may be considered for patients experiencing severe pain or disability due to an acute vertebral compression fracture. This procedure can stabilize the fracture and potentially denervate nociceptive fibers.
keyPoints: Most vertebral compression fractures occur at T11–L2, with lower lumbar region being second most common.
Up to 40% of cases may result in chronic pain if not managed appropriately.
Surgical consultation is warranted for nerve root impingement or spinal cord injury (<10%).
Vertebral augmentation can be considered for severe pain/disability from acute (<6 weeks) compression fracture.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.title: Role of RFA in Vertebrogenic Pain due to Endplate Inflammation
items.content: For patients with chronic axial lumbar pain where other spinal structures are less likely the primary source, and MRI shows Modic type 1 or type 2 changes, radiofrequency ablation (RFA) of basivertebral nerves can be considered. This approach targets vertebral endplate inflammation.
keyPoints: Vertebral endplate inflammation may be present in up to 40% of patients with chronic axial lumbar pain.
Presents similarly to discogenic pain and is most frequent in the lower lumbar area.
RFA can be considered when Modic type 1 or type 2 changes are demonstrated on MRI.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS
items.title: Mechanism of Trigger Point Injections for Myofascial Pain
items.content: Trigger point injections alleviate myofascial pain by improving local blood flow and facilitating the removal of inflammatory mediators, independent of the specific injectate used.
keyPoints: Pain relief mechanism involves vasodilation and removal of inflammatory mediators/cytokines.
No evidence supports superiority of any specific injectate (e.g., saline, anesthetic with or without steroids, botulinum toxin).
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.title: Natural History and Management of Radicular Pain
items.content: Most patients with acute radicular pain from a herniated disk improve within 3 months with conservative management, while spinal stenosis has a more guarded prognosis.
keyPoints: Acute radicular pain due to herniated disks typically improves within 3 months with conservative treatment.
Over two-thirds of herniated disks retract within 2 years.
Spinal stenosis often progresses with age, and its natural history is less favorable.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.title: Indications for Lumbar Nerve Root Decompression
items.content: Decompression surgery is indicated for severe or progressive neurologic deficits or refractory radicular pain, with mixed evidence on its superiority over conservative management.
keyPoints: Indicated for severe/progressive neurologic deficits or refractory radicular pain.
Meta-analyses show a small benefit of surgical decompression for spinal stenosis that diminishes after 2 years.
For herniated disks, short-term benefits are noted, but long-term advantages are questionable.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.question: What type of pain is characterized by abnormal processing of pain signals without evidence of tissue damage or pathology involving the somatosensory system?
items.explanation: The passage defines nociplastic pain as resulting from abnormal processing of pain signals without tissue damage or somatosensory system pathology.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of neuropathic pain compared to nociceptive pain?
options: It worsens with activities that stress the structures responsible for pain.
It often presents with focal neurologic findings such as loss of sensorimotor functions or reflexes.
It typically results from degenerative changes over time.
items.explanation: Neuropathic pain is associated with sensory abnormalities and focal neurologic findings, unlike nociceptive pain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP
items.question: What percentage of patients with acute nonradicular low back pain (LBP) experience pain at 3 months?
items.explanation: In one systematic review, 67% of patients experienced pain at 3 months.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP
items.question: What is the typical timeframe for herniated disks to resorb in patients with radicular low back pain?
items.explanation: Herniated disks typically resorb within 2 years.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for pain persistence in radicular low back pain?
items.explanation: Younger age is not listed as a risk factor for pain persistence.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of active trigger points compared to latent ones?
items.explanation: Active trigger points have more numerous, not fewer, vascular abnormalities.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.question: Which type of pain is most likely to be bilateral and worsened by sitting or bending forward?
items.explanation: Discogenic pain is more likely to be bilateral and worsened by sitting or bending forward.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.question: Which muscle group connects directly to the vertebral column?
items.explanation: Deep intrinsic muscles, such as semispinalis and multifidus, connect directly to the vertebral column.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a common feature in individuals with facet joint pain?
items.explanation: Individuals with facet joint arthritis may experience morning stiffness.

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.question: Which test has a sensitivity of approximately 80% for L5 and more caudad nerve roots in the context of radicular pain due to a herniated disc?
items.explanation: The straight leg raising test is noted for its ~80% sensitivity for L5 and more caudad nerve roots.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.question: What is the most common level affected by spinal stenosis?
items.explanation: The L4–5 level is the most common site affected by spinal stenosis, with a prevalence of 92%.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.question: Which symptom is a hallmark of neurogenic claudication in spinal stenosis?
options: Back pain that improves with activity
items.explanation: Neurogenic claudication symptoms are typically alleviated by leaning forward.

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.question: Which gait abnormality is most likely to suggest a central lesion?
items.explanation: A spastic gait is associated with central lesions.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.question: What does exaggerated lordosis potentially indicate?
items.explanation: Exaggerated lordosis can be secondary to a tethered spinal cord.

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.question: Which test is highly specific for diagnosing lumbosacral stenosis?
items.explanation: The crossed straight leg raising test has high specificity for lumbosacral stenosis.

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.question: Which condition is suggested by pain that disappears with sitting?
items.explanation: Pain that disappears with sitting is a strong predictor for lumbosacral stenosis.

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.question: Which of the following is considered a red flag in patients presenting with low back pain (LBP)?
items.explanation: Night pain is the most common red flag in patients with LBP, suggesting potential serious pathology.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.question: What imaging modality is considered the gold standard for detecting soft tissue abnormalities such as herniation?
items.explanation: MRI is considered the gold standard for detecting soft tissue abnormalities including herniation.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.question: Which imaging modality has sensitivity greater than 90% for identifying most lumbar pathology, including disk herniations?
items.explanation: Computed tomography (CT) has sensitivity >90% for identifying most lumbar pathology, including disk herniations.

items.question: What is the range of sensitivity for electrodiagnostic testing in patients with radicular pain and an abnormal neurologic examination?
items.explanation: Sensitivity rates for electrodiagnostic testing range from 51 to 86% in patients with an abnormal neurologic examination.

items.explanation: The specificity of electrodiagnostic testing ranges between 50 and 60%.

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: Which pharmacologic agent is no longer recommended as a first-line treatment for back pain due to its limited analgesic efficacy?
items.explanation: Acetaminophen is unlikely to provide significant analgesia for back pain and is no longer recommended as a first-line agent.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: What is the maximum duration acute low back pain (LBP) typically resolves without intervention?
items.explanation: Most cases of acute LBP resolve within 6 weeks.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: Which combination should be avoided due to increased risk of respiratory depression when treating back pain?
items.explanation: Concomitant use of opioids with benzodiazepines should be avoided due to the increased risk of respiratory depression.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a key benefit of interdisciplinary treatment programs for low back pain?
options: Better improvements in pain, function, and quality of life
items.explanation: Interdisciplinary treatment programs provide better improvements in pain, function, and quality of life.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.question: Which integrative medicine therapy has been shown to be slightly more effective than sham acupuncture for treating low back pain?
items.explanation: True acupuncture is slightly more effective than sham acupuncture.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.question: What is the effect of spinal manipulation on acute and chronic back pain compared to no therapy?
options: It has no effect at all.
It offers small benefits that diminish over time.
It is superior to other recommended physical therapies.
items.explanation: Spinal manipulation may provide small benefits for acute and chronic back pain, but the effects diminish over time.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.question: Which statement is true regarding the effectiveness of acupuncture for low back pain?
options: Acupuncture provides long-lasting benefits without continued therapy.
The effects of acupuncture are modest and short-lived without continued therapy.
Sham acupuncture is more effective than true acupuncture.
There is strong evidence supporting one form of acupuncture over others.
items.explanation: Acupuncture has been shown to be effective for pain, but the effects tend to be modest and short-lived without continued therapy.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.question: What is the diagnostic procedure used to confirm facetogenic pain?
items.explanation: The diagnosis of facetogenic pain is confirmed through diagnostic medial branch blocks.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.question: What is the primary treatment for extraarticular sacroiliac (SI) joint pain if intraarticular injections provide only transient relief?
options: Radiofrequency ablation of sacral lateral branch nerves
items.explanation: RFA of the sacral lateral branch nerves is considered for extraarticular SI joint pain if intraarticular injections provide only transient relief.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.question: Which procedure is associated with concerns about accelerated disk degeneration or injury?
options: Radiofrequency ablation of sacral lateral branch nerves
items.explanation: Discography is associated with concerns regarding accelerated disk degeneration or injury.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.question: Where do most vertebral compression fractures occur due to the transition from the thoracic spine to the lumbar spine?
items.explanation: Most vertebral compression fractures occur at the thoracolumbar junction due to the anatomical transition.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.question: What is a potential indication for surgical consultation in cases of posterior lumbar compression fractures?
options: Nerve root impingement or spinal cord injury
items.explanation: Surgical consultation is warranted in cases of nerve root impingement or spinal cord injury.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.question: Which types of changes on MRI are associated with vertebrogenic pain from endplate fractures or degeneration?
items.explanation: Vertebrogenic pain from endplate fractures or degeneration is associated with Modic type 1 and type 2 changes.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.question: What is the proposed mechanism of action for trigger point injections in treating myofascial taut bands?
options: Improving local blood flow via vasodilation and facilitating the removal of inflammatory mediators
Providing structural support to paraspinal ligaments
Increasing muscle mass around the affected area
items.explanation: Trigger point injections relieve spasmodic activity by improving local blood flow and facilitating removal of inflammatory mediators.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.question: Which injectate has been shown to be superior for trigger point injections?
options: Higher concentrations of local anesthetic with steroids
None, as no specific injectate has demonstrated superiority
items.explanation: Evidence does not demonstrate the superiority of any specific injectate for trigger point injections.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.question: What is a common cause of radicular low back pain?
options: Spinal stenosis or disk pathology causing nerve root compression
Facet joint degeneration without other spinal issues
items.explanation: Radicular low back pain is usually due to spinal stenosis or disk pathology causing nerve root compression.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.question: What is the natural history of pain from lumbar stenosis?
options: Most patients improve within 3 months with conservative management
Pain usually resolves spontaneously without treatment
Progresses with age due to causes like spondylolisthesis and facet hypertrophy
Responds well to epidural steroid injections in most cases
items.explanation: The natural history of pain from lumbar stenosis is more guarded as it often progresses with age.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.statement: Nociceptive low back pain (LBP) is typically progressive in nature.
items.explanation: The passage states that nociceptive LBP tends to be progressive, except for myofascial pain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.statement: Radicular pain always presents with neurologic deficits.
items.explanation: The passage notes that radiculopathy can occur without pain, and radicular pain may occur in the absence of neurologic deficits.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP
items.statement: In patients with chronic low back pain, most experience dramatic improvements after 6 months.
items.explanation: Stagnant rates of improvement are reported in chronic LBP, with few improving dramatically.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP
items.statement: Spinal stenosis typically resolves over time as the underlying pathology recedes.
items.explanation: Unlike disk herniations, spinal stenosis does not have an underlying pathology that recedes.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MECHANICAL LBP | RADICULAR LBP

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.statement: Myofascial pain is often associated with discrete trigger points that are easily palpable in the low back.
items.explanation: Trigger points are more challenging to palpate in the low back compared to the mid-back and neck.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.statement: Facet joint pain is typically unilateral and may be alleviated by sitting.
items.explanation: Individuals with facet joint pain are more likely to experience unilateral pain, and sitting may alleviate their symptoms.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.statement: The prevalence of asymptomatic disk herniation is lower than that of symptomatic lumbar disk herniation.
items.explanation: Asymptomatic disk herniation has a much higher prevalence, ranging from 29 to 43%, compared to the 1% annual incidence of symptomatic cases.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN
items.statement: Neurogenic claudication has high specificity for diagnosing spinal stenosis.
items.explanation: While neurogenic claudication has a sensitivity of 88%, it has low specificity for spinal stenosis.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 2) | RADICULAR PAIN

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.statement: Scoliosis can predispose patients to disk and facet joint degeneration.
items.explanation: Scoliosis is associated with an increased risk of disk and facet joint degeneration.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.statement: The straight leg raising test has high specificity for L5-S2 nerve root involvement.
items.explanation: The straight leg raising test has high sensitivity but variable specificity for L5-S2 nerve root involvement.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.statement: The absence of red flags in patients with LBP significantly decreases the likelihood of a neurologic emergency.
items.explanation: The absence of red flags does not meaningfully decrease the likelihood of a neurologic emergency.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: RED FLAGS | IMAGING
items.statement: A randomized trial demonstrated that MRI improves outcomes for lumbar epidural steroid injections (ESIs).
items.explanation: A randomized trial failed to demonstrate that MRI improved outcomes or meaningfully affected decision-making for ESIs.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · RED FLAGS | IMAGING

items.subtopic: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS
items.statement: Transitional anatomy is present in less than 10% of the population.
items.explanation: Transitional anatomy is present in 15–35% of the population.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS

items.subtopic: ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS
items.statement: Selective nerve root blocks (SNRBs) are performed similarly to transforaminal ESIs but avoid epidural spread.
items.explanation: SNRBs involve the blockade of only a single nerve root, avoiding epidural spread.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC TESTING | SELECTIVE NERVE ROOT BLOCKS

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.statement: Opioids have demonstrated significant long-term benefits for analgesia or function in chronic LBP.
items.explanation: Opioids have not demonstrated significant long-term benefits for analgesia or function.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.statement: Psychological conditions in chronic back pain patients are binary, either present or absent.
items.explanation: Psychiatric conditions exist along a continuum and are not simply binary.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.statement: Spinal manipulation is superior to other recommended physical therapies for treating low back pain.
items.explanation: Spinal manipulation is noninferior to other recommended physical therapies, but not superior.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.statement: Integrative medicine therapies for back pain are well-supported by high-quality studies.
items.explanation: The use of integrative treatments is characterized by low-quality studies.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.statement: Intraarticular and extraarticular pathologies of the sacroiliac joint have similar prevalence.
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of intraarticular and extraarticular pathology is similar.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES
items.statement: Discography has low false-positive rates in patients with prior spine surgery.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that discography is characterized by high false-positive rates, especially in certain populations like those who have undergone prior spine surgery.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · LUMBAR MEDIAL BRANCH NERVE BLOCKS AND ABLATION | SI JOINT INTERVENTIONS | INTRADISCAL INJECTIONS AND THERMAL-BASED THERAPIES

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.statement: Vertebral augmentation can be considered for patients with chronic pain due to vertebral compression fractures.
items.explanation: Vertebral augmentation is considered for severe pain or disability from acute (<6 weeks) compression fractures, not chronic pain.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION
items.statement: Facetogenic pain can result from the collapse of superior and inferior articular processes in posterior compression fractures.
items.explanation: Posterior compression fractures may cause facetogenic pain due to the collapse of these processes.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · VERTEBRAL AUGMENTATION AND BASIVERTEBRAL NERVE ABLATION

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.statement: Epidural steroid injections have consistently shown superior response rates for spinal stenosis compared to herniated disks.
items.explanation: Most studies have failed to show differences in response rates between spinal stenosis and herniated disks.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.statement: Lumbar nerve root decompression is indicated for severe or progressive neurologic deficits.
items.explanation: Decompression is indicated for severe or progressive neurologic deficits.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION
items.assertion: Nociplastic back pain is often termed 'nonspecific LBP'.
items.reason: It is characterized by diffuse pain, superficial tenderness, and pain patterns that deviate from normal neuroanatomy.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason correctly explains why nociplastic back pain is termed 'nonspecific LBP'.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · Low Back Pain | EPIDEMIOLOGY | PAIN CATEGORIZATION

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.assertion: Disc degeneration is associated with upregulation of inflammatory cytokines.
items.reason: Inflammatory cytokines sensitize intradiscal nerve endings and cause hypermobility, increasing mechanical stress on disks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)
items.assertion: The diagnosis of sacroiliac joint pain is made via anesthetic blocks.
items.reason: Anesthetic blocks have low false-positive rates for diagnosing sacroiliac joint pain.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false as anesthetic blocks are subject to high false-positive rates.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · NO CICEPTIVE PAIN (part 1)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.assertion: Nonorganic signs may signify underlying psychopathology.
items.reason: Nonorganic signs are associated with treatment failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)
items.assertion: Tenderness over the sciatic notch suggests piriformis syndrome.
items.reason: Freiberg's test assesses tenderness in this area.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAM (SEE ALSO CHAP. V8)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Targeted education, mindfulness-based stress reduction, operant therapy, biofeedback, progressive relaxation, and cognitive-behavioral therapies may benefit patients with back pain.
items.reason: There is stronger evidence for short-term than long-term benefits on pain and function from these psychological therapies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why these therapies can be beneficial.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Most studies have demonstrated one type of exercise as more beneficial than another for back pain.
items.reason: Yoga and Tai Chi are two commonly studied therapies that show consistent benefits.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; most studies have failed to demonstrate one type of exercise as more beneficial than another.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES
items.assertion: Most cases of low back pain can be attributed to one anatomic source.
items.reason: A thorough history and physical examination, along with appropriate imaging, can identify potential targets for interventional procedures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because most cases of LBP are nonspecific and cannot be attributed to a single anatomic source.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · MULTIMODAL AND INTERDISCIPLINARY STRATEGIES (part 2) | INTERVENTIONAL PAIN PROCEDURES

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.assertion: Disk arthroplasty is less invasive than lumbar fusion.
items.reason: Disk arthroplasty preserves motion better than circumferential or anterior fusion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)

items.subtopic: TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
items.assertion: Adjacent segment disease is more likely after lumbar interbody fusion.
items.reason: Fusion increases the risk of accelerated degeneration of adjacent disks and facet joints.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Low Back Pain · TRIGGER POINT INJECTIONS OF MYOFASCIAL TAUT BANDS | LBP WITH RADICULAR SYMPTOMS (RADICULAR LBP)
